lessons.title: What a Link Budget Is
lessons.content:
Imagine you send a radio signal across town. It leaves the transmitter strong, but along the way it gets weaker. Will it still arrive **strong enough to be understood**? A **link budget** answers exactly that one question. It is simply a tally: you start with the power you send out, **add** anything that helps, **subtract** anything that hurts, and see what power is left at the receiver.

Think of a **household budget**. Your income is money coming in; your expenses are money going out; what is left over is what you can spend. A link budget works the same way. The **gains** (helpers) are like income. The **losses** (things that weaken the signal) are like expenses. The signal **left over** at the receiver is like the money in your pocket at the end of the month.

What are the gains and losses on a signal's journey? You begin with the **transmit power** (how loud you shout). You **add antenna gain** — an antenna that focuses energy into a beam, instead of spraying it everywhere, makes the signal effectively stronger in the useful direction (you met this idea in the antennas sub-topic: *gain = focusing*). Then you **subtract the losses**: the signal spreads out and thins as it travels (this is **path loss**, which grows with distance — you met it in the propagation sub-topic), plus smaller losses from cables, walls and other obstacles, and bad weather like rain.

Add it all up and you get the **received power** — the strength of the signal arriving at the far end. If that number is large enough, the link works and the message gets through. If it is too small, the signal is buried and the link fails, just as a household "goes broke" when expenses beat income.

**Recap:** a link budget = transmit power + gains − losses = received power, and the whole point is to check whether that received power is *enough to be received*.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a link budget?
choices: A running tally of all the gains and losses on a signal's journey, used to find the power that arrives at the receiver
The exact price in dollars of building a radio transmitter
A list of the radio channels a device is allowed to use
The maximum number of users a network can support at once
items.explanation: A link budget adds up everything that helps a signal (gains) and subtracts everything that weakens it (losses) to find the received power, so you can judge whether the signal arrives strong enough. It is about signal strength, not money, channel lists, or user counts.

items.prompt: What single question is a link budget designed to answer?
choices: Will the signal arrive strong enough to be understood?
How much does the antenna weigh?
What color should the antenna be painted?
How fast does the radio wave travel?
items.explanation: The entire purpose of a link budget is to check whether enough signal survives the trip to be received and understood. The speed of the wave is essentially fixed and is not what the budget is for.

items.prompt: In the household-budget comparison, what plays the role of your monthly income?
choices: The gains: transmit power and antenna focusing that strengthen the signal
The losses: distance, cables, and rain that weaken the signal
The receiver's background hiss
The price of the radio equipment
items.explanation: Income is what comes in and helps, so it matches the gains (transmit power plus antenna gain). Losses are the expenses; the leftover signal is what is 'in your pocket' at the receiver.

items.prompt: In the same comparison, what plays the role of your monthly expenses?
choices: The losses: distance spreading, cable loss, obstacles, and rain
The transmit power you start with
The gain of the transmit antenna
The received signal that is left over
items.explanation: Expenses are what drain the budget, matching the losses that weaken the signal. Transmit power and antenna gain are income (gains); the leftover signal is what remains after expenses.

items.prompt: Where does a link budget START before adding and subtracting anything?
choices: With the transmit power (how strongly the signal is sent out)
With the receiver's noise floor
With the rain loss for the day
With the distance between the antennas
items.explanation: You begin with the transmit power, then add gains and subtract losses from there. The distance and rain are losses you subtract later; the noise floor is compared against the final received power.

items.prompt: Why does antenna gain count as a GAIN (a helper) in the budget?
choices: Focusing the energy into a beam makes the signal effectively stronger in the useful direction
It physically adds more electrical power to the transmitter
It removes all the rain in the path
It makes the radio wave travel faster
items.explanation: Gain means focusing: aiming the same energy into a narrower beam so it is stronger where you want it, like a flashlight reflector. It does not create new power from nothing, clear the weather, or change the wave's speed.

items.prompt: Which of these is a LOSS that you subtract in a link budget?
choices: Path loss from the signal spreading out as it travels farther
The transmit antenna focusing the beam
The receive antenna focusing the beam
The transmit power coming out of the radio
items.explanation: Path loss is the steady weakening as the signal spreads over distance, so it is subtracted. The two antenna focusing terms are gains, and transmit power is the starting amount you add to.

items.prompt: After you add gains and subtract losses, the number you get is called the received power. What does it tell you?
choices: How strong the signal is when it arrives at the far-end receiver
How much power the transmitter draws from the wall outlet
How many antennas the system uses
How long the cable between radio and antenna is
items.explanation: Received power is the strength of the signal at the destination, after the whole journey. You then check whether it is large enough for the link to work.

items.prompt: In the household-budget picture, what does it mean when the link "goes broke"?
choices: Too little signal is left at the receiver, so the link fails
The transmitter runs out of electrical power forever
The antenna physically breaks apart
The signal arrives too early to be useful
items.explanation: Going broke means the losses overwhelmed the gains, leaving too little signal to be understood, so the link drops. It is about signal strength running out, not hardware breaking.

items.prompt: Two ideas from earlier sub-topics feed straight into a link budget. Which pair is it?
choices: Antenna gain (focusing) as a helper, and path loss (signals weaken with distance) as a loss
The color of the antenna and the brand of the radio
The speed of light and the price of cable
The number of buttons on the radio and its weight
items.explanation: You already learned that antenna gain focuses energy (a gain) and that signals weaken as they spread over distance (path loss, a loss). The budget simply adds these up together with transmit power and other losses.

lessons.title: Decibels: Adding Instead of Multiplying
lessons.content:
Signals get *enormously* stronger and weaker on their journey — sometimes by factors of millions or billions. Writing those huge multiply-and-divide numbers is clumsy. So engineers use a friendlier unit called the **decibel (dB)**. The decibel is just **a compact way to write very big or very small ratios**, and it comes with one wonderful trick: in decibels you **add and subtract instead of multiplying and dividing**.

Here is the whole idea in a few anchors worth memorizing:

- **+3 dB ≈ double** (×2). The signal is twice as strong.
- **−3 dB ≈ half** (÷2). The signal is half as strong.
- **+10 dB = ten times** (×10).
- **−10 dB = one tenth** (÷10).
- **0 dB = no change** (×1).

Because dB add up, a big multiply turns into a small sum. Two +3 dB steps make +6 dB, which means ×2 then ×2 = **×4**. Add +10 and +10 to get +20 dB, meaning ×10 then ×10 = **×100**. A handful of decibels can stand for a gigantic change in real strength.

Now the payoff: a link budget done in decibels is **pure addition and subtraction**. Watch one simple add-up in words. Suppose you start with a transmit signal at **+20**. The two antennas focus the beam, adding **+6** of gain, bringing you to **+26**. Then distance steals a big **−100** of path loss, dropping you to **−74**. The cable nibbles another **−2**, leaving a **received level of −76**. That is the entire calculation: +20, then +6, then −100, then −2 — no multiplying anywhere, just a tidy running total.

This is why every link budget is written in dB. **Gains are positive numbers you add; losses are negative numbers you subtract**, and the final total is the received level you care about.

**Recap:** decibels turn painful multiplication into easy addition. Remember +3 = double, −3 = half, +10 = ten times, −10 = one tenth, and a link budget becomes one clean column of plus-and-minus.

items.prompt: What is the main everyday reason engineers use decibels (dB)?
choices: They turn huge multiply-and-divide ratios into simple adding and subtracting
They make radio waves travel faster
They measure the temperature of the receiver
They count how many antennas are in use
items.explanation: Decibels are a compact way to write very big or very small ratios, and their key benefit is that you add and subtract instead of multiplying and dividing. They are not about speed, temperature, or counting hardware.

items.prompt: Roughly what does +3 dB mean for the strength of a signal?
choices: About double (twice as strong)
About half as strong
Exactly ten times stronger
No change at all
items.explanation: +3 dB is the anchor for doubling. Half is −3 dB, ten times is +10 dB, and no change is 0 dB.

items.prompt: Roughly what does −3 dB mean for a signal?
choices: About half as strong
About double as strong
One tenth as strong
One hundred times as strong
items.explanation: −3 dB is the anchor for halving. Doubling is +3 dB, one tenth is −10 dB, and one hundred times would be +20 dB.

items.prompt: What does +10 dB mean?
choices: Ten times stronger (×10)
Twice as strong (×2)
Half as strong (÷2)
One tenth as strong (÷10)
items.explanation: +10 dB is the anchor for ten times. Double is +3 dB, half is −3 dB, and one tenth is −10 dB.

items.prompt: What does −10 dB mean?
choices: One tenth as strong (÷10)
Ten times stronger (×10)
Half as strong (÷2)
No change (×1)
items.explanation: −10 dB is the anchor for one tenth. Ten times is +10 dB, half is −3 dB, and no change is 0 dB.

items.prompt: You add two +3 dB steps to get +6 dB total. In real strength, what multiply does that stand for?
choices: ×4 (double, then double again)
×6 (add the two factors)
×2 (still just doubled once)
×10 (because the dB add up past 5)
items.explanation: Adding decibels means multiplying the real ratios: +3 dB is ×2, so +3 and +3 is ×2 then ×2 = ×4. You do not add the factors (that would give 6), and it is more than a single doubling.

items.prompt: Adding +10 dB and another +10 dB gives +20 dB. What real-strength change is that?
choices: ×100 (ten times, then ten times again)
×20 (just add the two tens)
×10 (still only ten times)
×2 (because anything over 10 dB is a doubling)
items.explanation: +10 dB is ×10, so +20 dB is ×10 then ×10 = ×100. Adding decibels multiplies the underlying factors, so a small dB sum can mean a giant change.

items.prompt: Work the budget add-up: start at +20, add +6 of antenna gain, subtract 100 of path loss, subtract 2 of cable loss. What is the received level?
items.explanation: Just add and subtract: 20 + 6 − 100 − 2 = −76. There is no multiplying; that is the whole convenience of working in decibels.

items.prompt: In a link budget written in dB, how do gains and losses appear?
choices: Gains are positive numbers you add; losses are negative numbers you subtract
Gains are subtracted and losses are added
Both gains and losses are always added
Gains and losses are multiplied together
items.explanation: The point of decibels is to make the budget a clean running total: helpers add (positive), things that weaken the signal subtract (negative). Nothing is multiplied.

items.prompt: A signal is boosted by +3 dB and then later cut by −3 dB. What is the net result?
choices: No overall change — doubling then halving returns to the start
Four times stronger
One tenth as strong
Twice as strong
items.explanation: +3 dB doubles and −3 dB halves, and they cancel: +3 − 3 = 0 dB, which means ×1 (no change). This is the elegance of dB — opposite steps simply add to zero.

lessons.title: Noise, SNR & Fade Margin
lessons.content:
So far we found the received power. But strength alone does not decide whether a link works. Every receiver also has a faint, ever-present **background hiss** called **noise**. It comes from heat jiggling the electrons in the circuits and from the electronics themselves. You can never get rid of it completely. This hiss sets a **noise floor** — the lowest level beneath which a weak signal simply gets lost in the murmur.

Two things make the hiss louder, and it helps to know them in words: noise **grows with temperature** (hotter electronics, more jiggling, more hiss) and noise **grows with bandwidth** (the wider the range of frequencies you listen to, the more hiss you let in). No formula needed — just remember: *warmer and wider both mean more noise*.

Here is the key insight. What matters is **not** how strong the signal is on its own, but how far it **stands above the hiss**. That comparison is the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**: how much louder the signal is than the noise floor. Picture hearing a friend in a **noisy restaurant**. Their voice is the signal; the crowd din is the noise. You can follow them only if their voice rises clearly above the din — raw loudness is useless if everyone else is just as loud. SNR is that gap, and **SNR, not raw signal power, is what decides whether the link works**. Plenty of gap → the message is clear. Too little gap → errors creep in, and if the signal sinks below the noise, the link drops.

Every receiver has a **sensitivity** (also called its threshold): the smallest signal — enough above the noise — that it can still make sense of. Your received power must beat this threshold for the link to close.

Finally, real conditions change. Rain, moving vehicles, and shifting reflections can all weaken the signal for a while. So good designers leave a cushion of extra signal above the threshold called the **fade margin**. On a clear day the signal sits comfortably high; when a rainstorm eats into the margin, the signal dips but stays above threshold, so your call does not drop. This same thinking scales everywhere: **Wi-Fi** (move closer or reduce interference for more margin), **cellular** (towers placed so phones keep enough margin while moving), and **satellite** links (large dishes and healthy rain margin because the path is so long).

**Recap:** noise is the floor of hiss (worse when warmer and wider); SNR — how far the signal stands above that floor — is the real decider; sensitivity is the threshold to beat; and fade margin is the cushion that keeps the link alive on a bad day.

items.prompt: What is noise in a radio receiver?
choices: A faint background hiss from heat and the electronics that is always present
A loud signal sent on purpose by the transmitter
The useful message after it has been decoded
The focusing effect of the antenna
items.explanation: Noise is the unavoidable background hiss produced by heat (jiggling electrons) and the receiver's own electronics. It is not a wanted signal, not the decoded message, and not antenna gain.

items.prompt: Two things make the noise hiss louder. Which pair is correct?
choices: Higher temperature and wider bandwidth
Lower temperature and narrower bandwidth
A longer cable and a shorter antenna
A faster wave and a brighter screen
items.explanation: Noise grows with temperature (more heat, more electron jiggle) and with bandwidth (a wider range of frequencies lets in more hiss). Warmer and wider both mean more noise.

items.prompt: What does signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) measure?
choices: How far the signal stands above the background noise
How fast the radio wave is moving
How many decibels of cable loss there are
How hot the transmitter gets
items.explanation: SNR is the gap between the signal and the noise floor — how much the signal stands out above the hiss. It is not about wave speed, cable loss, or transmitter temperature.

items.prompt: Why does SNR — rather than raw signal strength — decide whether a link works?
choices: A signal can only be understood if it stands clearly above the noise; if the noise rises to match it, even a strong signal is lost
Raw strength is impossible to measure, but SNR is easy
SNR makes the radio wave travel farther
Strong signals always fail, so weaker ones are preferred
items.explanation: What matters is the gap above the noise, not the absolute level. Like a voice in a loud crowd, a strong signal is useless if the hiss is just as strong; enough SNR is what lets the receiver pull the message out.

items.prompt: In the noisy-restaurant analogy, what plays the role of the noise?
choices: The crowd din around you
Your friend's voice
The words your friend is saying
The table you are sitting at
items.explanation: The crowd din is the background noise, your friend's voice is the signal, and you can follow along only if the voice rises above the din — that gap is the SNR.

items.prompt: What is a receiver's sensitivity (its threshold)?
choices: The smallest signal, far enough above the noise, that it can still make sense of
The largest signal it can ever receive without breaking
The amount of power the transmitter puts out
The speed at which it changes channels
items.explanation: Sensitivity is the weakest usable signal — the threshold the received power must beat for the link to close. It is a minimum, not a maximum, and has nothing to do with transmit power or channel speed.

items.prompt: For a link to work, the received power must be compared against what?
choices: The receiver's sensitivity threshold — it must land above it with enough SNR
The transmitter's weight
The length of the antenna in meters
The color of the cable
items.explanation: The final received power is checked against sensitivity: it must clear that threshold (with enough margin above the noise) for the message to get through. The other options are unrelated to whether the link closes.

items.prompt: What is fade margin?
choices: Extra signal kept above the threshold as a cushion for bad conditions like rain or movement
The total number of channels a radio supports
The hiss produced by the receiver's electronics
The gain added by focusing the antenna
items.explanation: Fade margin is a safety cushion of extra signal above the threshold, so that when conditions worsen (rain, moving vehicles, shifting reflections) the signal dips but stays usable. It is not a channel count, the noise itself, or antenna gain.

items.prompt: A rainstorm rolls in and weakens the signal. Why might the call still NOT drop?
choices: The fade margin gives enough extra signal that the dip stays above the threshold
Rain has no effect at all on any radio signal
The receiver's noise disappears in the rain
The transmitter automatically moves closer to you
items.explanation: Designers plan a fade margin precisely for events like rain: the signal weakens but, thanks to the cushion, it stays above the sensitivity threshold, so the link keeps working. Rain does weaken signals, and noise does not vanish.

items.prompt: Why do satellite links usually need large dishes and a healthy fade margin?
choices: The path is extremely long, so big focusing gain and a weather cushion are needed to keep enough signal above the noise
Satellites have no noise, so the dish is only for decoration
Short distances make signals too strong to handle
Rain makes satellite signals stronger, so margin is wasted
items.explanation: A satellite path is enormous, so a lot of path loss must be overcome with large high-gain dishes, and extra margin guards against rain. The same balancing of gains, losses, noise, and margin appears in Wi-Fi and cellular too, just at smaller scales.

items.prompt: Putting it together: a complete link check is best summed up as which sequence?
choices: Add gains and subtract losses to get received power, then compare it to the noise (SNR) and the threshold, keeping a fade margin
Multiply all the gains and losses together, then ignore the noise
Measure only the transmit power and stop there
Count the antennas and pick the heaviest one
items.explanation: The full picture is: tally gains minus losses for received power, then judge it against the noise floor via SNR and the sensitivity threshold, with a fade margin for bad days. Raw transmit power or hardware counts alone never tell you if the link closes.
